data.title: Brown-Forman: There's Enough Reason To Turn Neutral (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A spirits industry hangover continues to weigh on Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) as consumption in developed markets is still slowing; peer results underline a highly challenging operating environment. Yet, I believe that there are now clear silver linings in the investment as well—Brown-Forman’s emerging market gains are stabilizing the company’s sales, and the stock’s valuation finally reflects fair cash flow assumptions.

I maintained a Sell rating in my previous October 2025 article on the stock, titled “Brown-Forman: Stock Buyback Program Isn't A Buy Signal.” The stock has since lost a further -14% of its value; meanwhile, the S&P 500 (SP500) has declined by -2%.

The Spirits Industry Outlook Remains Dire

Brown-Forman’s industry environment remains very weak and uncertain. I have previously outlined the spirits industry’s struggles in a number of articles, and similar concerns were again echoed in the recent UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference. Brown-Forman’s CEO Lawson Whiting noted a continued decline in alcohol consumption and underlined both structural and cyclical reasons for declining consumption. Rising health consciousness, cannabis consumption, and weight-loss drug usage are more structural headwinds for alcohol demand, especially in developed markets, while weak spending power, especially among young people, pressures demand in a more cyclical manner.

It remains to be seen how much of the sharp decline in consumption will turn out to be cyclical. Some part of the decline should be transitory, as consumer confidence has been pressured in the past couple of years in the U.S. and Europe.

Concerningly for the industry, there still don't seem to be any signs of a consumption recovery after revenues started to decline back in early 2023. Diageo plc (DEO) had to lower its FY2026 guidance in February after first-half sales declined by -4%, and Pernod Ricard SA’s (PRNDY) sales declined by -5.9% organically in the company's most recent report. LVMH’s (LVMHF) wine & spirit sales declined by -9% organically in calendar Q4.

I believe that a prolonged consumption decline puts increasing weight on structural factors, strengthening the rationale to extrapolate current weakness over the long term.

Brown-Forman Has Remained Impressively Stable

Brown-Forman hasn’t been unaffected by weakening industry sales but has recently performed much better than peers; the company’s fiscal Q3 results even showed positive 1% organic revenue growth. Even though the growth figure is slow, any positive growth is, in my opinion, an impressive feat when considering the industry environment. Brown-Forman’s sales have continued to decline alongside peers in the U.S. and other developed markets, but market share gains in emerging markets and travel retail have stabilized total sales well.

Emerging market sales have grown by 15% organically year-to-date, with a strong performance especially in Brazil, Mexico, and Türkiye, among other markets. Many emerging markets still have much further market share potential for Brown-Forman, as the company noted in the 2025 Investor Day event; the Jack Daniels brand remains underrepresented in a number of countries. Continued momentum in emerging markets should continue to offset pressure in developed ones if Brown-Forman manages to expand Jack Daniels' brand reach effectively. Emerging market growth hasn’t sacrificed Brown-Forman’s margins so far, as organic operating profit stayed stable in Q3.

Ultimately, Brown-Forman’s growth outlook isn’t very encouraging despite emerging market potential. An uncertain consumption outlook still weighs on the company; even modestly positive growth is currently reliant on continued market share gains. Brown-Forman recently announced that it is bringing in Jim Peters as Executive Vice President and CFO and highlighted that Peters brings experience from Whirlpool Corporation (WHR) in managing margin pressures and volatile consumer demand. The company doesn’t seem to be positioning for much growth.

Brown-Forman’s Stock Valuation Finally Reflects the Outlook

I revised my discounted cash flow (DCF) model estimates very slightly for Brown-Forman. I again estimate revenue growth to stabilize at 2% from FY2027 forward and the EBIT margin to fall very slightly to 27.5% during the next few years.

I have slightly raised my cash flow conversion estimates as Brown-Forman has continued to moderate the company’s CapEx spend.

The estimates put Brown-Forman’s fair value at $23.3, very near the stock price as of writing. I believe that Brown-Forman’s stock finally reflects the company’s cash flow outlook fairly—previously high earnings multiples have moderated dramatically. The stock now only trades at a 13.6 forward P/E, compared to a 120% higher long-term average of 29.9.

The DCF model's fair value estimate is up from $19.0 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 8.00%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I now estimate a 4.78% interest rate, still 50 basis points above the 10-year bond yield. I have kept the long-term debt-to-equity estimate modest at 10%.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.28% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. Seeking Alpha now estimates Brown-Forman’s beta at 0.59. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 8.45% and the WACC at 8.00%.

Takeaway

I believe that there’s now reason for a more neutral stance on Brown-Forman. Even though the spirits industry remains in a concerning state, Brown-Forman’s emerging market growth has stabilized sales well during FY2026. Brown-Forman’s stock valuation also finally reflects fair cash flow growth expectations, making the long-term return profile much more balanced than previously. I upgrade my rating on BF.B and BF.A to Hold, but still note high caution due to industry headwinds.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VBR: Another March Selloff Makes For Another Small-Cap Value Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Global equities were flying high through much of February. The S&P 500, while the choppy laggard, ranged not far from all-time highs to begin the year. Global stocks soared, amid a stable dollar and optimistic outlooks for earnings trends. Domestic SMID caps also stood out in a good way, with shares of both mid- and small-sized companies posting solid alpha to the SPX. The macro-price action has shifted, however.

Today, I’m revisiting the Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF (VBR). Long the fund myself, I had a buy rating on the product back in August of 2025. Shares are up 4% since then, dividends included, which is a slight outperformance to the S&P 500. Today, with the fund in a 9% drawdown off its record high, I reiterate a buy rating. The valuation appears solid, while technicals point to potential support amid the current selloff.

Small Cap Value VBR +14% YoY

ETF Drawdown Heat Map: VBR -9% From Its 52-Week High

According to the issuer, VBR seeks to track the performance of the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index, which measures the investment return of small-cap value stocks and provides a convenient way to track the performance of a diversified group of small-cap value companies.

VBR is a large ETF, with $34 billion in assets under management as of March 18, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is low at just 5 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is materially higher than the S&P 500’s at 1.92%. Share-price momentum is rated somewhat poorly by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, which is not surprising, given the steep drop in recent weeks. I’ll outline key price levels on the chart to monitor later in the article.

As far as liquidity is concerned, VBR scores well. Average daily volume is north of 400,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at only two basis points, per Vanguard. Regarding risk, the product appears fine, but I’d call out that the small-cap value corner of the US equity universe is particularly exposed to perceived changes in the US macroeconomic landscape. Specifically, regional banks can play an outsized role in performance—if small business conditions are seen as deteriorating, VBR can lag. Today’s much flatter yield curve has done small-sized banks no favors lately.

But here's why now could be an ideal time, fundamentally, for VBR. Consider that the inflation shock from high oil prices has pressured interest rates higher. That dynamic--higher energy prices and costlier borrowing rates--hurts small-cap value companies' profit expectations more than those of other sizes and styles. But the forward oil curve shows much lower prices through the end of the year. If that plays out, the backup in Treasury yields should ease, setting the stage for a new leg of the VBR rally. Big picture, a healthy pullback was due after a torrid 10-month rally.

At the same time, we see an improvement in manufacturing PMI surveys, which serves as a fundamental bullish leading indicator for the most economically sensitive domestic firms. Finally, on the topic, I'm encouraged to see a continued rise in earnings forecasts. While we could see a profit-outlook dip, the broader trend appears to be higher after a protracted small-cap earnings recession.

Small-Cap Value Forward P/E Turns Lower vs History

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, gold-rated ETF by Morningstar plots in the lower-left section of the style box, indicating its bent to US SMID value. Still, there’s a material 29% mid-cap exposure, along with a small 3% large-cap position. There’s actually more diversification than you might think, including a very modest weight to the growth style.

On valuation, the 14.2x price-to-earnings ratio is compelling, particularly in light of the 10.2% long-term EPS growth rate. The resulting PEG ratio is low at just 1.4x. The P/E has turned more attractive amid the current equity drop, as earnings estimates remain on the rise.

VBR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming into the sectors, regional banks is not the only wildcard industry. In fact, the plurality of VBR’s holdings are found in the cyclical Industrials sector. So, manufacturing trends, employment levels, and domestic business investment are key factors.

Together, the top 3 sectors (Industrials, Financials, and Consumer Discretionary) command a high but not excessive 39% of the portfolio. And like most small-cap index ETFs, there’s low concentration in the top 10 holdings (which helps from a diversification standpoint).

VBR: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, we’ve seen volatile March stretches before. The good news is that over the past 10 years, the April through August period has been bullish on net. With a VIX close to 30 and VBR near correction territory, the groundwork may be set for a Q2 rebound.

VBR: Bullish Seasonal Trends Starting in April

The Technical Take

With a solid valuation and downward near-term price action, VBR’s technical situation remains on a decent footing. Notice in the chart below that shares are approaching the long-term 200-day moving average. We saw buyers step in on recent trips to the 200dma, and I would not be surprised to see it happen again. Of course, short-term momentum favors the bears, as evidenced by a weak RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. What’s more, there isn’t major support until VBR gets down toward the $193 to $201 range—a major point of polarity since late 2024.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the graph. Starting near $210, there should be ample demand for the ETF, given the high number of shares traded from there and lower. Furthermore, the 38.2% retracement of the April 2025 to February 2026 rally enters the picture at $206 (with the 50% retracement at $197). The upshot? I see a confluence of support 5-15% under today’s price, making for a favorable long-term entry on a risk/reward basis.

VBR: Shares Dip Toward Several Support Spots

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VBR. I see the US small-cap value index ETF as compelling on valuation, while support on the chart is not too far lower from here.

data.title: Verastem: Maintaining "Buy" As 1st Half 2026 Catalysts In Play
data.description:
Verastem Overview

The last time I spoke about Verastem (VSTM) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Verastem: Downgrading To Buy Based On RAMP-203 Setback In Targeting KRAS G12C NSCLC." With respect to this article, I noted that it had a setback in being able to further advance its phase 1/2 RAMP-203 study targeting G12C KRAS mutant non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC] patients. That was the bad news; however, the company pivoted over to developing its oral KRAS G12D ON/OFF inhibitor for the treatment of patients with solid tumors in an ongoing phase 1/2 VS-7375-101 study. The dose escalation portion of this drug, VS-7375, continues on at 1200 mg QD [once daily], and the combination portion of 600 mg with cetuximab presses on as well. However, the important update for this program is that it received guidance from the FDA to initiate disease-specific registration-directed phase 2 trials targeting patients with KRAS G12D mutated 2nd-line pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma [PDAC], 2nd-line/3rd-line non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC], and 2nd-line colorectal cancer [CRC] in combination with cetuximab. I had placed a "Buy" rating on this stock, and I'm going to stick to this rating because of its ability to initiate such registration-directed phase 2 studies for its VS-7375 candidate.

Not only that, but this candidate, known as GFH375, was evaluated in a phase 1/2 monotherapy study in China treating patients with KRAS G12D mutant advanced PDAC. These patients who took the recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D] of 600 mg QD achieved an overall response rate [ORR] of 41%. The beauty of GenFleet is that it already announced that it had initiated a phase 3 registration study of GFH375 for the targeting of pretreated KRAS G12D-mutated metastatic pancreatic cancer patients in China. With all this being said, Verastem is going to release data from its study of VS-7375 treating KRAS G12D mutant solid tumor patients in the 1st half of 2026. The second reason for the "Buy" rating is because the company had several abstracts accepted for presentation at the Society of Gynecologic Oncology [SGO] 2026 Annual Meeting on Women’s Cancers.

Specifically, the company is going to present long-term data from the phase 2 RAMP 201 study of avutometinib in combination with defactinib [AVMAPKI™ FAKZYNJA combination therapy] to treat patients with recurrent low-grade serous ovarian cancer [LGSOC]. This conference is being held between April 10th - 13th of 2026, and is a milestone for investors to keep an eye on. What I'm going to go over below, though, is the third reason for maintaining this "Buy" rating. It would be because the company announced completion of enrollment for its phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study using avutomeitinib plus defactinib in combination with gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel in 1st-line PDAC patients. It is believed that six-month expansion cohort safety/efficacy data is expected to be released in Q2 of 2026.

RAMP 205 Study Offers Another Avenue For Avutometinib Plus Defactinib Combiantion Regimen

As I noted above, there are several catalysts expected in 2026 for Verastem. One would be the abstract presentations accepted for data from the phase 2 RAMP 201 study using the avutometinib plus defactinib combination to treat patients with recurrent LGSOC. Plus, the continued proof-of-concept established by its partner GenFleet for VS-7375 [known as GFH375 in China] for the treatment of patients with KRAS G12D mutant solid tumors. Verastem is making headway with the advancement of this candidate alone, as it has already been able to align with the FDA to initiate several disease-specific, registration-directed phase 2 trials of its own. What I want to go over for this article is the program that deals with the development of avutometinib plus defactinib along with chemotherapy to treat 1st-line metastatic pancreatic cancer patients. Before going over this specific study program in detail, plus any catalysts to come from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this type of cancer is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Pancreatic cancer is characterized as the growth of malignant cells in the pancreas of a patient. However, the most common form would be that known as pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma [PDAC], where malignancy forms in the cells that line the ducts of the pancreas. This organ is found behind the stomach in the lower sector of it. It is responsible for producing enzymes to digest food. It is also responsible for producing hormones to keep a person's blood sugar levels in check as well. What makes it difficult to treat is that in the early stages of disease there are hardly any symptoms. In essence, it is not found until this cancer has reached late-stage disease. As a matter of fact, a huge majority of symptoms that these patients have don't happen until the cancer itself has already spread to other organs in the person's body. When symptoms for these patients are found, they can be:

  • Stomach pain
  • Weight loss
  • Fatigue
  • Jaundice
  • Diabetes that is hard to control

The global pancreatic cancer therapeutics market size is projected to reach $5.84 billion by 2030. This is a very good market opportunity, especially as, as I will be showing below, the company is going after 1st-line PDAC patients.

In order to see whether or not this combination would be successful in being able to treat these patients with 1st-line PDAC, the company is in the process of running the phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study. This particular trial is coming along well, because the company didn't really have to escalate doses further for it. What do I mean by that? Well, it was noted that Dose level 1 [DL1] would be used for these patients. This includes 2.4 mg of avutometinib and then 200 mg of defactinib. Along with the addition of 800 mg of gemcitabine and 125 mg of nab-paclitaxel as well. The dose expansion portion of this study enrolled a total of 29 patients for evaluation. The reason why I bring this up is because this creates a catalyst opportunity for investors to look forward to. The company expects to release 6-month safety/efficacy data from this phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study in Q2 of 2026. The thing is that this company has already been able to establish some proof of concept in a prior update at ASCO 2025. That's because out of 12 1st-line metastatic PDAC patients treated with this avutometinib + defactinib + chemotherapy combination, it was noted that 10 of them responded to it [83% overall response rate]. What is key, and remains to be seen, is if this number holds up with a larger pool of patients. A bright spot, though, would be the safety of this combination. Despite a patient receiving all of these drugs, adverse events [AEs] were noted to be manageable [Verastem Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #30]. Not only that, but patients were able to stay on the treatment itself, which is a very good thing.

VSTM Stock Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Verastem had cash equivalents and investments of $205 million as of December 31, 2025. However, in January of 2026 there was an exercise of warrants of $29.4 million. This puts a pro forma year-end cash of $234 million. The truth is that the company has a lot of cash on hand, and there is no need for dilution. That's because in its Q4 and full-year 2025 financial report, it notes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into the 1st half of 2027. The company has already received FDA approval of AVMAPKI FAKZYNJA CO-PACK and is producing net product revenue for it. This combination was able to produce net product revenues of $17.5 million for Q4 of 2025 and then $30.9 million for the full year 2025. Total operating expenses for the year ending December 31, 2025, were $201 million.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Verastem. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the upcoming abstracts that were accepted for presentation at the upcoming Society of Gynecologic Oncology [SGO] 2026 Annual Meeting on Women’s Cancers, April 10-13th of 2026. The goal is to showcase long-term data from the phase 2 RAMP 201 study using avutometinib plus defactinib to treat recurrent LGSOC patients. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that the long-term data to be shown from this study is going to be positive. Nor that the stock market is going to view such data in a positive manner, causing the stock price to trade higher.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing development of the company's oral KRAS G12D ON/OFF inhibitor VS-7375 in the ongoing phase 1/2 VS-7375-101 study treating patients with KRAS G12D solid tumors. It is expected that early data from this specific study will be released in the 1st half of 2026. There is no assurance that the data to be released from this study will be positive. Secondly, the company wants to develop the use of this drug in certain phase 2 disease-specific registration-directed phase 2 trials targeting patients with KRAS G12D mutated cancers, like PDAC, NSCLC, and CRC. The risk here is that upon completion of these studies, there is no guarantee that the results generated from them will be enough to warrant the company the ability to potentially file for regulatory approval of VS-7375 for one or more of these indications.

The third and final risk to consider would be that of the ongoing phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study using avutometinib and defactinib plus gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel to treat patients with 1st-line PDAC. Enrollment completion has been achieved, and it is expected that 6-month expansion cohort follow-up data from this trial will be released in Q2 of 2026. The risk here is that even though avutometinib plus defactinib has been shown to do well in treating patients with recurrent LGSOC, there is no guarantee that this combination along with chemotherapy will do well in the targeting of 1st-line PDAC patients. As shown previously in 12 patients, the company was able to achieve an 83% ORR. However, the risk is that a bigger sample size could end up altering the final outcome of the ORR to be produced.

Conclusion

Verastem may have had a setback before with its RAMP 203 study targeting patients with KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC patients. However, since then it has been making great traction with its candidate VS-7375 for the treatment of patients with KRAS G12D advanced solid tumors. Further evidence could be established soon in Q2 of 2026. Of course, that's only if the data ends up being positive from the phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study in the targeting of patients with 1st-line metastatic pancreatic cancer. With initial positive data from its study of VS-7375 in patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer from the RAMP 205 study, plus several expected milestones in 2026 that could potentially generate shareholder value, I believe it is important to maintain a "Buy" rating on this stock.

data.title: UIVM: An ETF That Offers Value, Momentum, And Low-Risk International Stocks
data.description:
Introduction to the VictoryShares International Value Momentum ETF

The VictoryShares International Value Momentum ETF (UIVM) is a $337M-sized product (in terms of assets under management) that has been in existence since October 24, 2017. UIVM is managed by VictoryCapital and issued under the brand name of VictoryShares. The firm is known for its rules-based niche ETFs. UIVM, which offers an annualized dividend yield of 3.3%, can be picked up at a net expense ratio of 0.35%.

What Does UIVM Do?

Principally, UIVM’s objective is to track an index called the Nasdaq Victory International Value Momentum Index (NVIVMI), which consists of almost 200 non-US stocks from 23 different international markets that are cheaply valued and have high momentum scores.

Given that this is a portfolio of global stocks that are not the most accessible and there are almost 200 stocks to cover, UIVM has gone down the more practical route of resorting to ‘representative sampling’ in its efforts to mirror the performance of NVIVMI, rather than just blindly replicating the latter. For those not familiar, representative sampling means choosing only a smaller portfolio of stocks (than the original index), which in aggregate have the same overall characteristics (be it the overall market-cap breakup, the risk-reward profiles, the sector exposures, etc.) as the underlying index. ETFs that resort to the sampling of their target indices are typically prone to seeing relatively high tracking errors, but we don’t believe one can level that accusation against UIVM, as its tracking errors are quite minimal (across different time frames) and a tad lower than what other ETFs generally experience.

How Is UIVM’s Underlying Index Built?

While UIVM may not necessarily replicate NVIVMI, it does attempt to ensure that 80% of its net assets consist of holdings from the latter. In that regard, it makes sense to study NVIVMI more closely.

NVIVMI’s starting universe is the Nasdaq Developed Markets ex United States Large Mid Cap Index (NDMeUMCI), and here, prospective stocks are initially assigned a value score and a momentum score. So how are these scores ascertained?

Well, the value score is a function of the stock’s inverse price-to-book value ratio, the inverse price-to-earnings ratio, and the inverse price-to-cash flow ratio.

With regard to the momentum score, the constituent stock’s price change over 6 months (excluding the most recent month) and the price change over 12 months (excluding the most recent month) are first ascertained before adjusting those metrics by the stock’s standard deviation for those periods (a measure of volatility). The purpose of this step is to pick out stocks that have trended well (over 6 months and 12 months) in a relatively linear manner, as opposed to those that have done well over said durations but experienced plenty of whipsaws during that period.

Once these scores are ascertained, ranks are then provided to each stock relative to all other stocks, with the intention of picking out the stocks with the relatively lowest value and highest momentum grades (this is on the basis of an aggregate score, where equal weights are assigned for both the value and momentum readings of each stock). Eventually, the stocks that finish in the top 25% are chosen to make up the target index and then weighted on the basis of their realized volatility (basically, stocks that are less volatile receive a higher weight in the index).

What Are the Key Features of UIVM's Portfolio?

UIVM, which covers almost 200 stocks, picks its holdings from 11 different sectors, although its top two sectors (financials and industrials) have an aggregate weight of 45% and are both cyclical in nature (prospects of these stocks will be closely related to the progression of the global GDP growth). Interestingly, the overcrowded tech sector garners the lowest weight of 3%.

From a geographical angle, UIVM covers stocks from 23 different global markets, an overwhelming chunk of which comes from developed markets alone (close to 87%). Within this pack, the bulk of the portfolio comes from stocks from the Euro region, while stocks from Japan and the UK account for the largest percentage weights at around 19% and 12%, respectively.

From a market-cap perspective, even though UIVM doesn’t weight its constituents on the basis of their respective market caps, this portfolio still tilts towards giant and large caps (with the latter dominating with a weight of 45%). Meanwhile, small-cap exposure is non-existent (after all, NVIVMI fishes for stocks from an index called the Nasdaq Developed Markets ex United States Large Mid Cap Index).

Stylistically, there’s no surprise to discover that UIVM largely tilts towards value-style stocks (58% of the portfolio), although hybrid stocks (those that offer relatively strong earnings and sales growth potential at reasonable valuations) also have a fairly decent weight of over one-fourth of the portfolio.

What Are the Risks Associated With UIVM?

Considering the weight of European, Japanese, and UK stocks in this portfolio, meaningful depreciation of the euro, the Japanese yen, and the pound sterling against the US dollar won’t reflect well on UIVM’s overall performance (even if those stocks do well in local currency terms), given that UIVM’s NAV (net asset value) is measured in USD terms.

Given the emphasis on certain value and momentum metrics that will naturally not remain static every month, this is a portfolio that would need to be rebalanced quite often (for context, UIVM’s tracking index gets rebalanced four times per year). What this means is that those who like to see relative stability in their portfolio holdings won’t necessarily get that with an investment in UIVM. In fact, over a year’s time frame, pretty much the entire portfolio gets turned over (99% annual churn), which is quite a contrast to your standard ETF, where not even one in three stocks gets turned over. Think of the elevated level of transaction costs that this product would incur (brokerage commissions and slippages between the bid and the ask) when you have close to 200 stocks that get churned in a year.

UIVM may have incorporated the word ‘momentum’ into its name, but that shouldn’t fool short-term players into believing that this is an ideal product to make a quick buck (the emphasis here is on momentum over 6 months and 12 months, by discarding the most recent monthly performance at the time of quarterly rebalancing). Also, the daily volumes that one sees in this counter (which is only around $0.5M) are not even a third of what most ETFs typically see ($1.6M). In addition to that, the average differential between UIVM’s bid and ask is not particularly tight and works out to 0.15%, which raises the prospect of slippage risks.

Despite offering some marginal exposure to some emerging markets like South Korea, South Africa, and Mexico (an aggregate weight of less than 13%), UIVM misses out on stocks from some of the notable BRIC nations such as China, India, Russia, and Brazil that serve as key cogs of the global economy (around 40% of global GDP).

Since UIVM’s top three sectors (which jointly account for well over half the portfolio) are all cyclical sectors, this makes this product quite vulnerable to weak global economic cycles.

Who is UIVM for?

Firstly, UIVM is meant for investors who are looking for a diversified portfolio of non-US developed market stocks that are relatively cheaply valued. As noted earlier, UIVM fishes for stocks from 23 different countries, and the portfolio as a whole is only priced at an earnings multiple of 12.4x and a book value multiple of 1.17x; this is in stark contrast to an ETF that tracks the FTSE All-World ex US Index that is priced at much higher earnings and book value multiples of 15.6x (an implied relative premium of 26%) and 2.1x (an implied relative premium of 90%), respectively!

Considering the level of turnover that it incurs and the rules that UIVM’s tracking index follows in building a portfolio of stocks from distant shores and global exchanges, we feel UIVM’s expense ratio of 0.35% is also quite reasonable (the median ETF expense ratio is 0.5%) and will appeal to cost-conscious ETF investors

UIVM’s emphasis on long-term trending stocks while dismissing those that have only begun trending over the most recent month (at the time of quarterly rebalancing) also ensures that investors who pursue UIVM aren’t getting a portfolio of stocks that have only seen a temporary boost.

By also incorporating a volatility facet to the momentum score and then also weighing the individual constituents on the basis of their respective volatility quotients, there should be fewer stocks that are up based on a temporary or short-term rally.

While UIVM’s nomenclature would suggest that it would largely only appeal to value-conscious investors and those who like trending stocks, we also feel that those who like ETFs that pay regular income could likely also fancy UIVM. Unlike most equity ETFs that distribute either quarterly, semi-annually, or annually, UIVM follows a more frequent monthly distribution schedule. Interestingly, those monthly distributions also annualize to a yield figure (3.3%) that is better than the ETF median of 2.8%.

Investors who appreciate ETFs that don’t have a high degree of concentration in certain individual stocks, or a bunch of stocks, may also be drawn to UIVM. Unlike most ETFs that follow market-cap weighted indices (which typically result in a high degree of top-heaviness), UIVM’s tracking index is weighted on the basis of its constituents’ relative volatility quotients. Only eight out of the 200-odd stocks from UIVM have a weight of over 1%, and jointly the top 10 only account for less than 11% of the portfolio (whereas for the median ETF, top 10 concentration is 4x higher at 44%).

Those who like old-world and cyclical industries like financials and industrials and want to reduce exposure to sectors like technology will also likely appreciate UIVM.

UIVM's Peers

Since UIVM employs quite a unique rules-based process that emphasizes value, momentum, and then assigns weights based on the volatility profiles of its chosen stocks, finding another ETF alternative that also follows the same process may be hard to source.

Having said that, we’d also like to mention two other international ETF products that employ similar factors as UIVM when choosing their stocks and also have a lot of similar characteristics (be it country exposure, market-cap exposure, top 10 weights, or cyclical sector dominance).

The first is the Goldman Sachs ActiveBeta International Equity ETF (GSIE), which is older than UIVM and a lot more popular (look at the exceptional AUM and daily dollar volumes in the table below). The second is the WisdomTree International Multifactor Fund (DWMF), which is the most low-profile product out of this lot (daily dollar volumes of only around $120K and AUM of ~$37M).

Contrary to what, GSIE’s name may imply, it isn’t an actively managed ETF but rather tracks an index called the Goldman Sachs ActiveBeta International Equity Index. The goal of GSIE is to pick out international stocks that not only score highly on value and momentum but also on quality (such as high return on equity or return on asset readings). Like UIVM, there is also an emphasis on low volatility stocks. GSIE also offers much wider coverage (it covers close to 650 names), is less prone to change than the other two (annual churn of only 22%), and is the cheapest out of the lot at an expense ratio of 0.25%. GSIE’s drawback is its yield, which is the lowest.

DWMF is the only actively managed ETF from this bunch, and like UIVM, it also covers only around 200 stocks (with a similar level of churn) while emphasizing value and momentum. However, besides value and momentum, it also looks at quality. Interestingly, rather than focusing on volatility like the other two, UIVM looks for international stocks that also have lower correlation than the broader international markets. DWMF also differs from the other two in that it doesn’t consider Canadian stocks and tilts mainly towards industrials (rather than financials). However, it is also the priciest product out of the lot. Both DWMF and GSIE fall short of UIVM’s dividend profile, both in terms of frequency and yield.

Summary

UIVM is a non-US developed market-focused ETF that not only emphasizes stocks with low value and high momentum quotients (by combining value and momentum, UIVM also diminishes the risk of getting locked with value traps) but also those with relatively low-risk profiles. UIVM does not come across as concentrated, and its monthly distribution facility is also an added bonus. However, investors should also note that this is a portfolio that sees significant churn across a year and offers limited exposure to exciting and high-growth pockets like emerging markets and technology stocks.

This article answers these three main questions about UIVM:

  1. What are the key features of UIVM's portfolio?
  2. What type of investor is UIVM suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  3. How does UIVM compare to other international ETFs that also emphasize value and momentum factors.

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Copa Holdings: I Believe This Is A Potential Diamond In The Rough
data.description:
If you follow my work, you know I almost never cover airline companies. I consider them, for their operating model, to be almost entirely uninvestable - and I'm not the only investor feeling this way. Airline companies combine some of the worst fundamentals of a business model in one "package," and it takes a lot to get away from this.

The combination of massive CapEx (the purchases, financing, or even operation of aircraft) coupled with razor-thin margins since the deregulation of most airline industries worldwide has created a situation where these companies spend very heavily but still can't make much of a profit in terms of the ROIC. Furthermore, while some investors may argue that some airlines are differentiated - I myself will, for instance, argue that Qatar Airways is (my carrier of choice for 90% of flights) - usually those differentiated airlines are not investable due to state ownership, or their differentiation is a momentary rather than a permanent thing.

The further combination of sensitivity to external/macro factors (as we've seen not once, but now 3-4 times in the last 25 years) has the potential to drive these companies down with the force of a hydraulic press. During times of downturn, these companies' costs don't simply disappear either - they still need to maintain their assets, be they airplanes, catering, airports, or staff. So the combination of these fundamentals, coupled with a very limited ability to build any sort of competitive advantage, is not something that is, simply put, very appealing to put money into.

Put another way, for me to consider investing in a company like this, the company would have to present me with a reason or set of reasons why its operations are differentiated enough to generate alpha over time.

And I think, if not finding one, that is, I think I have found an airline that might be differentiated enough to justify the purchase at a certain price.

And that airline is Copa Holdings, S.A. (CPA).

Copa Holdings - What Makes a Differentiated Airline

An airline that would be attractive to me would operate in a market where they hold a lot of sway - a market that is either underserved or where the player has upside and a market that has growth potential. It's therefore likely to be an emerging market.

We have that here. Copa Holdings operates out of Panama, has a market capitalization of just above $4.5B even after the latest drop, and has what I consider to be acceptable leverage. It pays a current yield of 6.2%, and it's listed on the NYSE.

I promised a differentiated carrier. So what exactly makes Copa differentiated, given that plenty of carriers offer yields?

The Carrier works out of Panama City. Panama City is a strategic logistical, political, and financial hub between North and South America. It's further exposed, therefore, to emerging markets with significant growth rates. Excluding the price of airline fuel, which the company can do nothing about, barring running its own refinery, Copa Holdings operates one of the lowest cost bases in the entire industry. It is this operating margin upside that I believe to be one of the major differentiators. Copa has consistently managed, with exceptions for disruptions, excellent margins.

This is one of the things that flagged the company for me originally. This sort of trend is not something common in the airline industry. Our own native carrier, SAS, has operated mostly in the negative margins since COVID-19, and even before, it didn't often manage to go above 4%. And while some NA carriers compare, they don't operate with the same geographic focus nor under the same risk profile.

So what makes Copa Holdings so capable of achieving better margins?

It's a combination of lower labor costs, extremely efficient operations (similar, I would argue, to how a carrier like Qatar is built), the utilization of Panama City as a major hub that can combine both short-haul and long-haul (unlike, for instance, Doha), and its expanding and impressive LATAM network. Obviously, asset specificity is also a part of it. The company operates a fleet of reliable, comparatively easy-to-service Boeing 737 planes - no other planes at all. It has a flight completion rate of over 99.7%.

So far, so good. We can see some differentiation. Hopefully, this has at least piqued your interest a bit.

I have been through the company's balance sheet and filings from bow to stern. I will now address the company's recent share price weakness, which also piqued my interest. In my view, it's coming from rising costs, which are pressuring margins. Q4, the latest results, missed EPS forecasts by close to 5%, which is what I believe caused the stock declines here.

Furthermore, the company seeks to grow its fleet, potentially breaking its asset tradition. Risk #2. Risk #3 that I was able to garner from the numbers is that while EBIT and operating margins are very solid, the conversion to FCF suffers, with the past few years showing a decline.

I won't say that exposure to Panama is a risk - I believe Panama and its role as a hub to be entrenched enough to rise above it - nor is it specifically a balance sheet risk.

Overall, I had to dig a bit to get to this because the company's leverage is currently very low. On a net basis, it has less than $1B of debt, which, compared to the asset side of the balance sheet, is much lower (even if much of the asset side is planes, not cash). However, the company has plenty of cash on hand - almost $400M at the end of 2025, and therefore stands strong in the face of these risks.

The expansion of the fleet is going to be done with similar assets as they already own.

The key focal point for me to convey is profitability. This is where CPA differentiates itself from other investments. And while costs are going up, the combination of geographical appeal, coupled with labor cost advantages, coupled with already-existing efficiencies, coupled in turn with the growing role of emerging markets in LATAM, leads me to a high conviction that these advantages will remain.

Beyond the balance sheet/filing risks I have mentioned above, with leverage below 0.7x also not being a risk, I do not view any risk as likely to destabilize this company over a long period of time, barring something like COVID-19.

There are those mentioning the dividend coverage being a risk. I do not consider this to be accurate - the payout ratio, even at sub-40% or close to 40%, is safe as long as the company's capital allocation strategy remains in place. The Q4 '25 miss is not something I'd lose sleep over because of load factors well above 85% and strong profit. It's outperforming most of its peers and competitors that operate in this region.

For another carrier to replicate CPA's structure and potential would take decades - if it's even possible (which I, personally, doubt it would be). The sheer growth potential and fundamentals found here are solid enough to take away from the difficulty typically associated with investing in airlines.

So, as to the question of what makes this company different and investable, I would answer that (well-founded) query with the following.

Aside from obvious solid fundamentals and a good yield, I refer you to the company's operating assets, accuracy/safety record, profitability, and labor cost advantages, but for growth, it's exposure to one of the best-growing geographies in the world. It goes entirely in line with my investing more in emerging markets.

Some analysts may mention the impact of the ownership structure - a small group of shareholders controls CIASA, which in turn holds a 20%+ stake in the company - but I do not consider long-term stability-oriented decisions (which these shareholders have shown that they will prioritize) to be a bad thing over short-term return-oriented decisions.

I, therefore, say that at the right price, fundamentals and operating characteristics justify my rating of Buy.

So what is the "right price?"

Valuation for Copa Holdings - Arguably, It's Here

The fact is that we have seen a significant drop in company valuation. In less than 1.5 months, Copa Holdings has moved from a peak of above $150/share to below the share price seen about a year ago in 2025, due, I would argue, to the Q4 '25 results. Copa Holdings currently trades at an average P/E of 6.61x. This is below the average for the company. The company's 5-year average is at 7.46x, and its 20-year average is above 11x. Given that CPA has shown its ability over the past 20 years to grow at well above 11% per year in terms of AEPS, I do not consider this to be an illogical or inflated valuation average.

Consequently, I would apply at the very least a 7.5x P/E to the company, and more fairly a P/E of 9x. I also consider the company's growth estimates to be at least somewhat indicative, given its beat average of 45% on a 1-year basis with a 10% margin of error. Airlines are volatile, but for the reasons mentioned above, I view the growth likelihood of this geography (LATAM and Panama as a hub) as high.

I, therefore, do not mark the company down for its exposure but consider the 7-8% AEPS growth forecast to be likely, compared to 11.2% for the 20-year average (meaning it's already lower than it used to be).

I further argue that P/E is the valuation tool to use for Copa Holdings because the company is consistently profitable, making earnings-based valuation ideal. There is cyclicality, but this is event-driven. I view the multiple as effective to get "beyond" this.

With my justification out of the way, I say that at 7.5x P/E, the company has a fair value of at the very least $150/share for 2028E. That means that at the current share price of $110/share, with the yield of 6.2% considered, the company is likely to generate 19.2% per year, a standard calculation for forward yield and return. If put at the 20-year average, that return would instead be 36.3% annualized, coming to a triple-digit return with a fair value of $238/share. My earlier-stated average would instead come in at 26.38% per year, with an average FV of $188/share for 2028E.

So where's the truth?

I'd argue the truth, or the "right" target, is about $190/share in 2028E. It takes into consideration the growth and maintenance of the company's profitability. If you wanted to be even more conservative than I am at 7.5x P/E, you could go with a P/E of 6.5x P/E, but at that point you, as the investor, would be arguing that a class outperformer should be traded at single-digit P/E for reasons of (I would assume) LATAM exposure, volatility, and the fact that it is an airline.

I consider that argumentation to be insufficient.

While things certainly could go lower here (things can always go lower), this in itself does not prevent me from saying that Copa Holdings is an attractive prospect here. At any of my fair value targets, the current price of $110/share is too low. I put my price target, or PT, for the company at $122/share (the price at which you get a 15% annualized return or above to my FV) for the 9x P/E - and the cheaper you buy, or the higher you estimate (or the better the company does), the better your return will be.

I therefore rate Copa Holdings as a Buy with a PT of $122/share and an FV of about $190/share for 2028E. The title "diamond in the rough" makes sense here given my usual stance on airlines.

Thesis

Copa Holdings is what I would consider to be something rare. It's an airline that I consider to be qualitative and profitable enough to rate a Buy as a conservative, value-oriented investor. I cite reasons related to historical performance, geography, growth potential, fundamentals, and good efficiencies.

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of $190/share, which in turn comes to a €$122/share price target ("PT") to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is now doing. I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a Buy rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a valuation target of €140/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Buy rating as of March 2026.

data.title: Ethereum Price Holds Near $2,130 After Fed Message Cools Rebound Hopes
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​Ethereum (ETH-USD) is being traded around $2,130 this Thursday, March 19. It was able to lift above the overnight low for some time during the day, but the rebound turned inevitable as markets continued to digest the Federal Reserve stance that offered no relief for risk assets.

Ethereum no longer looks comfortably range-bound. What had started as a pause above $2,300 has turned into a lower trading shelf, with price now leaning on the $2,100 area and testing whether buyers still have the appetite to absorb dips.

The chart does not show full-scale capitulation, but it also does not show much urgency from the long side. Bounces in price have been brief, and the market keeps fading before it can rebuild any rhythm above the closest resistances.

A fall under $2,100 would leave traders looking toward the $2,050 region first and then the deeper $2,000 marker. If price manages to recover from the current value, the first zone that needs to give way sits around $2,200, while a stronger repair would only start to look credible closer to $2,280 and above.

Monetary policy and the hunt for yield

The broader macroeconomic environment continues to dictate the pace of Ethereum’s recovery. By maintaining the current federal funds rate while acknowledging that the path to 2% inflation remains bumpy, the central bank has effectively sidelined the most aggressive bears.

This “wait-and-see” stance from policymakers has stabilized the U.S. Dollar Index, which in turn has relieved the downward pressure on dollar-denominated digital assets that characterized Wednesday’s trading session.

Internal network dynamics are also playing a role in the current price floor. The total amount of Ethereum participating in staking protocols has reached a new all-time high of 30% of the total supply. This massive reduction in liquid, exchange-available tokens is creating a structural supply crunch that competes with macro-driven selling.

Even as speculative interest wanes, the consistent demand from institutional staking providers offers a unique yield-based value proposition that distinguishes Ethereum from other non-productive assets.

Geopolitical tensions in energy-producing regions remain a wild card that could disrupt this stability at any moment. The oil prices recently rising typically act as a tax on global growth and drive inflation up, which could force the Fed into an even more restrictive posture later this year. Investors are considering these external risks against the internal growth of Ethereum, specifically the anticipated efficiency gains from the upcoming Glamsterdam network upgrade scheduled for later this quarter.

Mapping the path to $2,600 or $2,000

In a more positive scenario, if geopolitical tension eases and a softer run of labor market data becomes reality, that could help bulls to push Ethereum back toward the $2,500 mark. If the token can transform the $2,400 resistance zone into support, the next major target would be near $2,580, the higher point in 2026 at this point. That move would likely come with money rotating out of defensive assets like gold and back into risk-sensitive markets as expectations for higher rates begin to soften.

Meanwhile, if reports confirm that service inflation is a real danger again, Ethereum could face another sharp leg downward. A fall below the $2,250 price region means the market is starting to see a potential prolonged period of a tighter monetary policy, which could end the current rally. Under that scenario, the next key support area may be around $2,050-2,100, where buyers have previously absorbed selling.

Ethereum functions as the primary settlement layer for the decentralized economy and serves as a benchmark for institutional confidence in blockchain technology. Its ability to maintain value during periods of central bank uncertainty highlights its evolving role as a sophisticated financial instrument.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - Wall Street Gaps Down, Weak Dip-Buying Attempts
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The past day's FOMC session wasn't easy on investor sentiment, as a coordinated oil–petrodollar–hawkish–repricing attack played war on stock bulls.

The downtrend in global indexes is now more severe – the pricing out of cuts, leaving the way open for hikes in central banks, was confirmed by the streak of hawkishness resounding in rate decision press conferences.

The Swiss National Bank, Bank of Japan, Bank of England, and European Central Bank confirmed once again that the rise in energy commodities and supply shocks would bar the way for any soft prints in prices and that, without accounting for the direct effect of oil price rises on goods, they have also raised concerns about economic growth. Global central banks are now back to hawkish.

It's been difficult to justify buying stocks in this environment – See how global stock benchmarks have struggled.

One particular element that did not help risk sentiment was that Brent prices, which track more closely with ex-American energy prices, have largely decoupled from WTI – Brent spiked back to $116 per barrel overnight and holds right around $110, creating a new wave of anxiety around the globe.

On the North American side, however, WTI oil is holding below $100 per barrel, and this has been helping to contain the market open.

What is sure, at least, is that metals haven't liked the new rise in Brent the slightest – gold is down 3% from the previous session and now down 15% from its $5,400 war highs.

The previous session's news around attacks on Iranian and Qatari/Saudi energy infrastructures created the gigantic movement and divergence in commodities.

As long as WTI doesn't form a definite break above $100 and holds above, the outlook for US equities should remain more rangebound. More details on our index analyses below.

Note: Ironically, as I am writing this piece, oil is attempting a rebound towards $101 from the latest breaking news. Trump TACO'ed again; the US won't ban crude exports in retaliation from the lack of allies participation to liberate the Strait of Hormuz.

This will weigh on stocks, particularly if the rally in WTI continues.

US indexes are trying their best to rebound on support, but they will need to form a more decisive rebound from current levels; below them, there is space for much tougher downside.

Let's spot where today's rough price action is heading by looking at today’s mid-session charts and post-FOMC trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

Except for energy minerals and industrial services, the entire market heatmap is getting sold off from the hawkish repricing and lower growth projections.

Tesla (TSLA) is getting hit the hardest, along with producer manufacturing and electronic technology (was the tech rebound a dead-cat bounce??)

Dow Jones 1H Chart and Trading Levels

The DJIA is now standing at a significant support at its August 2025 highs area and war bear channel lows.

  • Rebounding here would point to a test of the 47,000 resistance and channel top
  • Extending the correction below 45,710 would lead to a bear channel break and further downside ahead.
    • To get a better idea for the direction, check out the highs of the 1H Doji and lows of the channel to play for breakouts.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance levels

  • 46,060 Doji highs (intraday bullish above)
  • March 8 war lows pivot 46,300
  • Resistance 47,000 +/- 100 points (channel top)
  • Pivotal Resistance 47,500 to 47,650
  • Key Resistance at 48,000
  • 48,400 to 48,500 mini-resistance

Support levels

  • 45,700 to 45,900 August support & channel lows) – bearish below
  • August highs 45,715
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 1H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq is still holding its textbook 24,200 to 25,000 range in the latest action.

Attempting a rebound from support, bulls will face similar Doji tests as seen in the DJIA:

  • Above the 1H Doji, the range will be confirmed to hold
  • Below the morning gap lows (24,150), expect further downside in the index
    • 24,000 will first come as a test.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance levels

  • 1H Doji 24,390 (bullish above)
  • 24,450 to 25,550 range pivot (short-term resistance)
  • Mini-intraday resistance 24,750
  • Key resistance 25,000 to 25,200 (range highs – long-term bullish above)

Support levels

  • February support 24,150 to 24,200 (range lows – bearish below)
  • October - November support 23,800 to 24,000
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 support

S&P 500 1H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is now also facing a critical test ahead, having formed a double bottom right below 6,580.

  • Rebounding here would be necessary to relaunch better hopes in US stock markets.
  • Breaking the morning lows and double bottom, however, would open the door for a much larger correction!
    • Check out the past hour 1H Doji highs (6,620) for confirmation – If broken, next intraday resistance would be found at 6,740.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance levels

  • Momentum pivot 6,640
  • 6,680 to 6,700 mini-resistance
  • 6,740 key intraday resistance
  • Pivotal resistance 6,770 to 6,800

Support levels

  • 6,570 to 6,600 Monday key double bottom support
  • Overnight lows 6,567
  • 6,490 to 6,512 October lows immediate support
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades and keep track of WTI prices!

Original Post

data.title: Brookfield: Assets Under Management On A Record Ascent
data.description:
The growth of Brookfield's (BN) assets under management ("AUM") is set to underpin sustained revenue, fee-related earnings, and multiple growth for the firm even as the market gets jittery around private credit and the impact of AI on the software industry. BN's most important asset is its 73% ownership stake in alternative asset manager Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), a Manhattan-based firm that fundraised $35 billion during the fiscal 2025 fourth quarter. This formed a record quarter for capital formation, with the total for the full year at $112 billion, raising BAM's total AUM to $1.2 trillion and fee-bearing capital to $603 billion. This grew by 12% year-over-year, a nominal growth of $64 billion, as BAM continues to build what I'd describe as an extremely sticky and long-dated recurring revenue base. This limited reliance on volatile carry or transaction-driven income.

While BAM has dipped by 31.43% from its 52-week high, BN has so far dipped 20.82%. This disparity is broadly unique, with the performance of both securities typically tracking each other closely. It implies BAM being oversold or a downside ahead for BN on a reversion to the mean. BN last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.06 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $0.24 per share annualized to drive a 0.61% dividend yield. BAM provides the enhanced income choice, with its quarterly dividend just hiked 15% to $0.5025 per share, annualized for a 4.57% dividend yield. BAM is the higher-income, lower-beta play. This has been inverted since the high-profile September collapse of First Brands Group and Tricolor sparked fears of "credit cockroaches" and pushed private credit into what I've termed a manufactured crisis.

BAM has $280 billion of fee-bearing capital in credit, around 46.4% of the total. Fee-related earnings ("FRE") came in at $867 million, up 28% from $677 million in the year-ago comp, and advancing from $754 million in the third quarter. The figure was also ahead of analyst consensus by $46 million. The consistent growth of FRE represents the foundations of BN's ability to create long-term shareholder value, as growth in FRE, in aggregate with the dividend yield, will underpin total returns for the firm. Fee revenues came in at $1.51 billion, with FRE margin expanding by 300 basis points sequentially to 61% from 58% in the year-ago quarter. This was on the back of better control of costs, with the sustained rise in AUM and fee-bearing capital driving greater operating scale, with total direct costs of $621 million. Credit and Real Estate form the largest strategies for BAM's FRE, with the asset manager

Distributable Earnings and the Private Credit Risk

BN's most salient risk factor will come from the ongoing private credit "crisis." This has so far been defined by aggressive redemption requests from private credit funds, with the worst of the bearish analysis coalescing around equating the risk of the asset class to the subprime lending crisis that sparked the 2008 financial crisis. Critically, for BN, a reduction in capital formation cadence would force down a multiple to FRE that currently stands at 22.95x against $3.08 billion against FRE for its full fiscal year 2025. Fourth quarter distributable earnings ("DE") at $767 million, around $0.47 per share, grew by 18% from its year-ago comp, with DE coming in at $2.7 billion for the full year. DE tracks FRE, which in turn hinges on fee-bearing capital formation that is reflective of healthy demand across BAM's five core strategies of (1) Credit; (2) Real Estate; (3) Private Equity; (4) Renewable Power & Transition; and (5) Infrastructure.

There has been an upswing in FRE margin, with this set to continue to drive DE and to underpin further dividend hikes. Further, private credit risks seem to be overstated. There really hasn't been a crisis-worthy spike in default among U.S. corporates. The Proskauer Private Credit Default Index came in at 2.46% for the fourth quarter of 2025. This was up sequentially by 62 basis points from 1.84% in the third quarter, a modest rise with Proskauer describing the U.S. credit market as healthy. Bears might also flag that the selloff might be looking towards a possible AI-driven rise in software-related defaults as vibe coding leads to the commodification of software. BN generated fourth-quarter non-GAAP EPS of $0.67, with total revenue growing by 3.8% year-over-year to $20.16 billion. The company's DE before realization grew by 11% on a per share basis to $0.63 per share on the back of BAM's outperformance.

Conclusion

I am rating BN as a "Buy." The manufactured private credit crisis will eventually give way to a rational evaluation of the long-term impact of AI on software as bears continue to chase the elusive credit cockroaches that are meant to herald a new financial crisis.

data.title: A Look Around Markets In A Scary Post-FOMC Morning - Market Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Yesterday's FOMC meeting marked the beginning of a rough session for traders around the globe.

Global stock indexes took large hits, correcting down 3% in Japan, London, and Europe and gapping down starkly in this morning's open.

Dow Jones Index 4H chart – Source: TradingView

As you can see on the index chart for the Dow Jones Industrial Index, heavy selling pressure from a massive hawkish repricing has applied large pressure on US equities and others around the world.

Yesterday's conference from Jerome Powell did not help to contain the initial scare of higher rates for longer – as a matter of fact, his wordings around the current 3.50%-3.75% were estimated to be located "at the plausible range of neutral [rates]", implying that further movement in the Fed policy would only be contingent on a weakening economy or labor market.

And we haven't seen any sign of this for now – quite the contrary: jobless claims came at 205K, the lowest since January (and they were at two-year lows at that time)

This also combines with a flurry of central bank decisions (including the ECB, Bank of England, Bank of Japan and the Swiss National Bank), which haven't communicated any type of dovishness.

When assuming that crude oil prices are now 40% higher than they were the last trading day of February and close to 90% higher since their January lows, it is difficult not to reprice some heavy form of inflation spike.

Everybody feels it at the pump; it hasn't been fun.

Market Reactions

Crude oil spikes higher but eases since, Brent-WTI spread widens even more

European and Asian markets are getting even more concerned by the latest escalation in energy infrastructure attacks across the Middle East, as these regions remain heavily affected by supply disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.

A detailed crude oil analysis will be coming at the top of the hour.

US Treasuries volatility spikes, curve flattens

Bond traders are now largely undoing the steepening that was priced in the rate curve (implying that rates will be lower in the near term than the long term).

The issue with this is that such curve conditions tighten credit and largely hurt banks.

This is also the type of curve movement that can precede either rate hikes or recessions – two conditions that aren't the most favorable for stock markets or any other markets, as a matter of fact.

In general, a flatter yield curve means that current inflation is expected to be higher than long-term inflation – this comes with the heavy rises in WTI.

The US dollar marks a top after the FOMC

Markets may at least reconcile with the fact that the US dollar is now correcting back to its pre-FOMC levels – this happens at the top of its July 2025 range (from 96.00 to the current 100.00).

An in-depth US dollar analysis will be coming up this afternoon, but the general theme to keep in mind is the fact that an easing dollar may also ease general pressures around markets since the beginning of the war.

Watch the 4H 50-MA that has, however, just acted as support – bouncing here would reinforce the pre-open panic.

This pullback has helped the JPY to relief, and similarly for other currencies – the USD is now at the bottom of the FX board in today's session. The correction in WTI is also helping.

Gold retests its February lows

The price action in gold and other metals has been very ugly, as participants really price out the global rate cuts, giving relative strength to yielding assets.

This phenomenon is magnified by the lack of consistent rallies since the beginning of the war and a general scare of a long-term inflationary process.

Still, it's not the end of the world for metals until $4,400 breaks – here could be a decent, small DCA entry point for those who did not participate in the trend.

Below $4,400, however, may see further correction back to $4,000 and below, $3,500, so make sure to watch for your size and add only on confirmation!

Cryptos are tumbling

Bitcoin is now back below $70,000; Ethereum remains relatively strong at $2,100, but the overall market is getting hurt by the latest hawkish repricing.

Keep a close eye on headlines and flows throughout the day, as today is promised to be quite volatile.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Gold Price Retreats Toward $4,629 After Fed Hold And Oil Shock Tighten Macro Squeeze
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) fell again on Thursday, March 19, with spot bullion trading near $4,630 as traders reacted to a tougher rate backdrop a day after the Federal Reserve stayed on hold. The metal has not been able to turn the geopolitical stress into a durable bid, with rising energy costs and firmer rate expectations instead pulling money away from non-yielding assets.

Gold is now pressing against an area that matters. The slide toward $4,629 has left the market sitting near its weakest levels since early February, and the tone remains heavy after a run of steady daily losses.

The chart no longer looks like a pause inside an uptrend. It looks more like a market still shedding length, with rebounds staying brief and sellers showing up before price can rebuild above nearby resistance around $4,700.

Momentum also appears tired rather than washed out. If gold goes down from the $4,600 zone in a clear way, the next point of interest could emerge closer to $4,500. To slow the pressure, bulls likely need a recovery back through the $4,700-4,750 area first.

Safe haven logic runs into rate reality

Normally, a geopolitical shock of this size would hand gold a cleaner lift. This time the reaction has been more complicated because the same conflict pushing up demand for safety is also driving oil sharply higher and reviving inflation anxiety.

That has kept the rate story front and center. With the Fed holding policy steady at 3.50-3.75% and showing no clear rush to deliver easier settings, the opportunity cost of holding bullion has become harder to ignore.

The broader cross-asset picture has added to that pressure. The prices for energy have jumped all over the world. Global risk appetite has weakened, and markets have started to recalibrate around a world where central banks may need to stay restrictive for longer than many had hoped only a few weeks ago.

What could shift next

A stabilization scenario is still on the table. If oil gives back part of its latest surge and Treasury pricing begins to reflect a less-threatening inflation pulse, gold could find room to repair and work back toward $4,700, with a stronger recovery opening the way to the $4,800 area.

The opposite path is easier to see for now. If energy prices remain elevated and traders keep pushing out the timing of policy easing, gold may remain under pressure, with a break below $4,600 exposing a deeper move toward $4,500.

The current gold market feels like a standoff. While geopolitical tensions keep people from selling gold entirely, the high cost of holding it in a high-rate environment is preventing any serious rallies from taking hold.
